content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 25814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Compliance • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660          322 5660          322 5901          322 6441          322 6444

December 19, 1979

Mr. Mitchel B. Kahn
City Attorney
3200 Cochran Street
Simi Valley, CA 93065

Dear Mr. Kahn:

     Thank you for your letter dated November 20, 1979, seeking advice on behalf of Simi Valley City Councilmember Bill Baker regarding the disqualification provision of the Political Reform Act. The following advice is provided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b). The facts, as I understand them, are as follows.

     Mr. Baker recently earned $52,000 from the sale of real property contingent on the building of a post office on the site. Before a joint meeting of the city council and planning commission a discussion ensued about the proposed location of the post office. Councilman Baker announced his conflict of interest and did not participate in the discussion. When Councilman Baker attempted to speak on the matter as a private citizen, he was advised not to do so by your office.

     The question you have asked our office to consider is whether Councilman Baker, after announcing his conflict of interest in a particular decision, is precluded from further participation in that decision as a private citizen. Government Code Section 87100 provides:

          No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows he has a financial interest.

     The Commission has provided by regulation that,

          Making or participating in the making of a governmental decision shall not include:

          Appearances by a public official as a member of the general public before an agency in the

Mr. Michel B. Kahn
December 19, 1979
Page Two


course of its prescribed governmental function to
represent himself or herself on matters related
solely to his or her personal interests.

Government Code Section 18700(d)(2).

Councilman Baker cannot use his official position to influence
or participate in governmental decisions in which he has a
financial interest. However, Councilman Baker may participate
in his capacity as a private citizen in those proceedings
which are open to the public so long as he is representing
his own personal interests.

I hope that this advice is helpful. If I can be of
further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Dwight E. Dickerson
Legal Counsel
Legal Division

DD:kp

79349

CITY OF SIMI VALLEY
3200 COCHRAN STREET, SIMI VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 93065
(805) 522-1333

November 20, 1979

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
Conflicts of Interest Division
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

It would be extremely helpful to myself and the members of
our council if you could analyze the following fact situation
and supply us with an opinion, either formal or informal in
nature, which responds to the issue addressed. Any assistance
you can give us in this matter would be most appreciated.

Facts: Councilmember Baker, as a realtor, earned or is in
the process of earning a $52,000 commission on the sale of certain
real property for the construction of an United States Postal
Service building on Cochran Street, near Galena Avenue in the City
of Simi Valley. At a joint meeting of the Planning Commission and
the City Council, called at the request of the Planning Commission
to seek policy guidance in the process of reviewing the City's
General Plan, a discussion ensued concerning the proposed location
of the post office building. Mr. Baker promptly stated that he
had a conflict of interest and declined further participation in
the discussion. He then left his chair and proceeded to a chair
in the audience. Whereupon he attempted to speak to the issue
as a "citizen" and not as a councilmember. Upon the advice of the
City Attorney, Mr. Baker promptly cut off any further remarks
concerning the subject.

Issue: Once Mr. Baker had stated his conflict and abstained
from further discussion concerning the subject of his conflict,
was he then free to leave his chair and address the Council and
Planning Commission on the same subject but in the capacity of
a "citizen"? (There is no way of determining what Mr. Baker may
have said had he continued to speak, and whether or not it related
to a particularly personal matter regarding his commission or
whether it related to the post office site in general.)

CATHIE WRIGHT, Mayor
DAVID REES, Mayor Pro Tem
BILL BAKER, Councilman
ELTON GALLEGLY, Councilman
GREG STRATTON, Councilman

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 20, 1979
Page 2


An additional issue arises, the answer to which may or may not
vary the opinion. That is, would the result vary if Mr. Baker were
addressing only the Planning Commission or only the City Council
on the same subject?

Thank you very much for your time and your consideration of this
important issue to our City. If you have any further need for additional
facts or information, please feel free to either call or write this
office and we will make whatever additional information available as
you may require.

I might point out for your information that this letter is being
prepared with the knowledge of Mr. Baker, the Mayor, and the City Manager.

Very truly yours,


Mitchel B. Kahn, City Attorney

MBK:bl

cc: Mr. Baker
    Mayor Cathie Wright
    City Manager, Lin Koester

sections.question: Once Mr. Baker had stated his conflict and abstained from further discussion concerning the subject of his conflict, was he then free to leave his chair and address the Council and Planning Commission on the same subject but in the capacity of a "citizen"? An additional issue arises, the answer to which mayor may not vary the opinion. That is, would the result vary if Mr. Baker were addressing only the Planning Commission or only the City Council on the same subject?
sections.conclusion: Councilman Baker cannot use his official position to influence or participate in governmental decisions in which he has a financial interest. However, Councilman Baker may participate in his capacity as a private citizen in those proceedings which are open to the public so long as he is representing his own personal interests.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city councilmember who has disclosed a financial conflict of interest and abstained from participation in a governmental decision may subsequently address the same decision-making body on the same subject matter in a private citizen capacity.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A public official who has disclosed a conflict of interest may speak as a private citizen in public proceedings related to that conflict, provided they are not using their official position to influence the decision and are merely representing their personal interests.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (dated November 20, 1979) and the FPPC response (dated December 19, 1979). Response signed by Dwight E. Dickerson, Legal Counsel. Minor OCR artifacts present in document but core content is legible. References Government Code Sections 87100 and 87102(d)(2).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Once Mr. Baker had stated his conflict and abstained from further discussion concerning the subject of his conflict, was he then free to leave his chair and address the Council and Planning Commission on the same subject but in the capacity of a "citizen"? An additional issue arises, the answer to which mayor may not vary the opinion. That is, would the result vary if Mr. Baker were addressing only the Planning Commission or only the City Council on the same subject?

CONCLUSION: Councilman Baker cannot use his official position to influence or participate in governmental decisions in which he has a financial interest. However, Councilman Baker may participate in his capacity as a private citizen in those proceedings which are open to the public so long as he is representing his own personal interests.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that Councilman Baker, after disclosing a financial conflict of interest in a post office development project, may participate as a private citizen in open public proceedings concerning that matter, but cannot use his official position to influence the governmental decision.
